content.problemStatement:
The summer is coming and Chef wants to fill his swimming pool with water so that he could enjoy swimming in it during these hot days.

The swimming pool is NNN metres in length (long course) and a fixed width 111 metre. Its long course is split into NNN equal parts, with possibly different depths. For each valid iii, the iii-th part has depth DiD_iDi​ metres.

You should process QQQ queries. There are two types of queries:


1  x  v1\; x\; v1xv — pour vvv cubic metres of water into the xxx-th part of the pool
2  x2\; x2x — find how much metres deep is the water surface from the ground in the xxx-th part of the pool. If there's no water on this part then the water surface depth is same as the depth of this part.
Water level obeys the following rules. As long as the water level in some part of the pool is higher than the water level in at least one of the adjacent parts, the water from it will move to adjacent parts with lower water levels until water levels become equal or there is no water left in this part. If the adjacent part from both sides (left and right) have lower water level then the **half of the quantity of water in the current part will move to left and the other half will move to the right**. The water on the leftmost part can't move to the left, and the water on the rightmost part can't move to the right.

When the water level reaches the surface of the pool (i.e. its depth become 0), the pool overflows and the water level does not increase anymore.

Please refer to the samples for more clarification.
constraints: The sum of N in all test cases does not exceed 10^5
The sum of Q in all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and Q. The second line contains N space-separated integers D1, D2, …, DN. Q lines follow. Each of these lines describes a query in the format 1 x v or 2 x.
content.outputFormat: For each query of the second type, print a single line containing one real number — the depth of the water surface of the x-th part of the pool if it contains water, or Dx if it does not.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: The pool is divided into 5 parts, the depths of the parts are 3 5 2 1 6, in first query we pour 2 cubic units of water into 4-th part, since both 3-th part and 5-th part are deeper than 4-th part then the water can't stay in 4-th part, 1 unit will go to left and 1 unit to right. the 1 unit that went to the right stayed in 5-th part and made its depth equal to 5, the 1 unit that went to the right couldn't stay in 3-th part because 2nd part is deeper than it so it moved to 2nd part and made its depth equal to 4.

Explanation: The pool is divided into 5 parts, the depths of the parts are 3 5 2 1 6, in first query we pour 2 cubic units of water into 4-th part, since both 3-th part and 5-th part are deeper than 4-th part then the water can't stay in 4-th part, 1 unit will go to left and 1 unit to right. the 1 unit that went to the right stayed in 5-th part and made its depth equal to 5, the 1 unit that went to the right couldn't stay in 3-th part because 2nd part is deeper than it so it moved to 2nd part and made its depth equal to 4.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we will simulate the water flow in the pool. We will maintain an array to store the current depth of water in each part of the pool. When a query of type 1 is encountered, we will add the given volume of water to the specified part of the pool and then simulate the water flow to adjacent parts until the water levels become equal or there is no water left in the current part. When a query of type 2 is encountered, we will simply print the current depth of the water surface in the specified part of the pool.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * Q * N) — because in the worst case, we might need to simulate the water flow for each query and for each part of the pool.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we need to store the current depth of water in each part of the pool.
